summary:
Otto von Bismarck's tenure as Minister President of Prussia (1862–1871) and then as Imperial Chancellor of Germany (1871–1890) was one of the most consequential political careers in modern European history. His 'blood and iron' speech of September 1862 — in which he declared that 'the great questions of the time will not be resolved by speeches and majority decisions... but by blood and iron' — articulated the realpolitik philosophy that guided his approach to [[event:2534|German Unification]] and European diplomacy.

Appointed Minister President by King William I in a constitutional crisis over military reform, Bismarck proved utterly ruthless in pursuing his ends by any means available. He governed Prussia in defiance of parliamentary budgetary control, crushed liberal opposition, and pursued an aggressive foreign policy designed to isolate Austria and make Prussia the dominant German power. His manipulation of the Schleswig-Holstein crisis, the engineering of the [[event:2575|Austro-Prussian War]], and the provocation of the [[event:2535|Franco-Prussian War]] demonstrated a mastery of diplomatic deception and calculated risk-taking that astounded contemporaries.

After unification in 1871, Bismarck's statecraft changed character. As chancellor of the new German Empire, he became a conservative force seeking to preserve the international order he had created. His elaborate alliance system — the League of Three Emperors, the Triple Alliance, the Reinsurance Treaty with Russia — was designed to keep France diplomatically isolated and prevent a two-front war for Germany. He also pioneered the welfare state in Germany to undercut socialist support, introducing health insurance, accident insurance, and old-age pension systems in the 1880s.

Bismarck was dismissed by the young Kaiser Wilhelm II in 1890, who rejected his cautious alliance management and allowed the Reinsurance Treaty with Russia to lapse. The subsequent Russian turn toward France and the eventual emergence of the Triple Entente vindicated Bismarck's warning that his alliance system's dissolution would leave Germany surrounded by hostile powers — the nightmare scenario that materialized in 1914.
